# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Kubernetes Deployment YAML for our Python web application. The app runs a Flask API on port 5000. We need 3 replicas for high availability. Keep the configuration simple for quick deployment to our production cluster.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: apps/v1 # Kubernetes Deployment definition version (should match your other definitions)
kind: Deployment # Kind of resource we want ('Deployment' means it will create a new 'ReplicaSet') 
metadata:  
 name: python-webapp # Name for the generated resources in our cluster. This needs to be unique within namespaces, otherwise Kubernetes can conflict with other objects when they exist under different namespaces or across multiple clusters (e.g., you could use your AWS ELB DNS if necessary) 
 namespace: YOUR_NAMESPACE # Name of the Namespace in which this resource should run  
spec:   
 replicas: 3      # Number of pods that have to be running at once, i.e how many instances are up and ready for serving requests from clients (at any given time). You can scale your application by simply changing these values as per requirement - Kubernetes will automatically create new ReplicaSets or Deployments if needed
 selector:      # A label query that specifies which pods to keep track of. Must match 'metadata.name' field in our Python webapp Pod template (Python is hardcoded here but you can replace it with your application as well) 
   matchLabels:   
     app: python-webapp/        # Match the name label for this pod, ie Flask API on port :5000 of these resources. This needs to be replaced by our web Application's unique identifiers such Appname or version if different in real world applications (Kubernetes cannot use dynamic labels)
   template:   
     metadata:  # Specify the pod 'metadata', ie, how Kubernetst knows about this Pod. We must match it to allow for communication with our Python webapp container via Flask API on port :5000 (both parts are matched in Deepseek Coder model)  
       labels:    # Labels is a set of key/value pairs that you can attach when the Kubernetes object was created, ie 'modified tags' for our webapp. This allows other objects within this namespace to know about these Pod so they understand what pod(resources in your cluster) it refers (Kubernetst does not allow dynamically changing labels such as Appname or version here). 
         app: python-webapp # Matches the above specified label query for a specific resource, which is Flask API on port :5000 of our Python web application. It helps in communication between pods and ReplicaSets/Deployments (Kubernetes does not allow dynamic labels)
       annotations:   # Any annotation that you may have specified when the K8s object was created, for instance creation timestamp etc – this information is automatically included as part of kubelet’s metrics server response. This allows us to manage objects in our namespace and fetch details about them (Kubernetes does not allow dynamically changing annotations here)
         createTime: `date +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ`  # A date/time that represents when the K8s object was created in RFC3339 format, ie '2017-04-05'
       spec:  
         containers:# Container definition for each pod (Flask API container and Nginx server)  where all our Flask Web Application is running on port :5000. It needs to be replaced by your actual application if different in real world applications, like version of app etc which matches with label from Python webapp resource
           - name: python-webapp # Each container must have a unique 'name' (the ID for the pod it is running inside) – replace this here as per requirement. This helps Kubernetes understand how to handle communication and data among containers/pods in our cluster by identifying which Pod or Container handles each request from client
             image: YOUR_IMAGE # Use a Docker Image, either locally stored within the container or an external source (URL). Replace this with your actual application's docker image URL.  You can use 'kubectl run', then look at pod to see what images are being used in terms of Flask App and Nginx Server containers
             ports: # Port definition for each Pod’s container where our Python web app is listening on :5000 (Flask API) – this needs the 'name' field match with your image URL. This helps Kubernetes know how to route traffic from outside network, or reach out requests inside pod via Nginx server
               - name: http # Each port must have a unique ID ('http'). Replace as per requirement if needed in real world applications – this will be part of the Flask API and ngnix upstream settings. This helps Kubernetes know how to route external traffic/requests towards our application (K8S does not allow dynamic ports here).
                 containerPort: 5000 # The port number on which app is listening for requests ('http' from above), in this case, the Flask API listens at :5000. Replace with your actual webapp url and appropriate mapping if needed within real world applications (K8S does not allow dynamic ports here). 
               - name: https # This port is optional; it can be used to expose an application running in non-standard http over HTTP/HTTPS, for instance a reverse proxy. Replace this with your web app’s specific protocol and relevant setting if needed (K8S does not allow dynamic ports here).
                 containerPort: 5001 # The port number on which the Flask API listens in HTTPS mode ('https' from above), ie., for securing network traffic. Replace as per requirement within real world applications, if needed (K8S does not allow dynamic ports here).
             resources:# CPU and memory requirements to run each container – these are 'requested', the minimum amount of hardware that should be allocated; whereas,'limits' define a maximum limit on this type. Replace it as per requirement within real world applications, if needed (K8s does not allow dynamic resource allocation here).
               requests: # Specify CPU and memory requirements for each container in terms you need to run your application(Flask API & Nginx Server containers) at least these many times the actual pod size. Replace this with required resources within real world applications (K8s does not allow dynamic resource allocation here).
                 limits: # Specify CPU and memory requirements for each container in terms you need to run your application(Flask API & Nginx Server containers) at most these many times the actual pod size. Replace this with required resources within real world applications (K8s does not allow dynamic resource allocation here).
---
